text: The surgeon performs laparoscopic hernia repair using the eye palm technique on an irreducible hernia. Adhesions are visible between the omentum and the anterior abdominal wall. An assistant aids in reducing the hernia. The medial umbilical ligament on the left side is cut, and additional access to the adhered omentum is created. The Vipro II mesh is fixed and sutured to the peritoneum at the inferior edge.

The surgeon separates the omentum and pushes it towards the upper abdomen, revealing the hernia defect. A telescope is introduced to visualize the left side indirect inguinal hernia. A Vipro-II mesh is inserted and spread over the anterior abdominal wall, covering the triangle of dome, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster. The mesh is positioned to extend six centimeters beyond the defect margin. The outer crowning of the mesh is secured with absorbable tackers lateral to the triangle of pain to avoid capturing nerves, minimizing postoperative pain. The mesh is further secured over the arc of the transversus abdominis and the Cooper ligament using tackers, ensuring firm attachment.

The surgeon positions the Vipro-II mesh over the anterior abdominal wall, ensuring it covers the rectus abdominis muscle. Care is taken to avoid firing the absorbable tacker directly into the bladder, instead placing it medial or lateral to the medial umbilical ligament. The mesh is secured above the inguinal ligament, avoiding the trapezoid of disaster, triangle of doom, and triangle of pain to prevent incarceration of the omentum or bowel.

The surgeon performs the inner crowning of the mesh over the inguinal ligament, securing it with absorbable tackers to ensure it covers approximately six centimeters beyond the defect margin. Extreme caution is exercised while suturing the peritoneum at the inferior margin, using a Dundee jamming knot to secure the mesh without penetrating deeper structures. The suturing continues along the inferior edge of the mesh, ensuring only superficial bites of the peritoneum are taken to avoid damaging underlying vessels and the ureter.

The surgeon carefully sutures the mesh parallel to the peritoneum, ensuring each suture is locked to prevent any space for momentum or bowel incarceration. The suturing is performed near the iliac vein and artery, with attention to avoid these vessels. The mesh is secured without blood loss or dissection issues, and the procedure aims to prevent complications such as hydrosil formation, recurrence, fistulization, or seroma.

The surgeon pulls the peritoneum towards themselves to safely separate it from the external iliac artery, allowing for the inferior surface of the mesh to be securely sutured, covering the vessel completely. The peritoneum is mobilized, especially on the left side if the sigmoid colon is uncooperative, ensuring the mesh covers all structures without anything anterior to it. The suture passes through the peritoneum at least one centimeter internally, effectively holding and traveling through the peritoneum to lock the mesh in place.

The surgeon employs a double muffing technique to secure the mesh in place, ensuring it adheres tightly to the peritoneum. The suturing process involves passing the suture through a loop to create two layers, providing additional pressure and stability to the mesh, while avoiding puncturing the iliac vessels to prevent severe bleeding.

The surgeon performs an abutting termination by taking a deep bite in a safe area without muscle, using a loop within loop technique to lock the suture. The needle is carefully managed to avoid coming out, ensuring a double lock. The excess suture is cut, and the needle is removed, completing the procedure.
